speaker: interviewee
text: And you know it's very interesting. From time to time, we all think of why did we marry someone? And I always hope that people get married because they love each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what is love? What is love? Howard and I had a friendship. We, we had a good relationship. I liked him. And maybe he's-- maybe he is right. Maybe I did want to get out of the house, and, and it was with someone that I liked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whoever, whoever might listen to what I'm saying, in retrospect, now that it's 40-- 43 years after, I wouldn't have changed a day of it. So I've been one of the fortunate ones that, even so, we might argue over all kinds of things. And he is very critical of me. I tell you, sometimes, the criticism that goes on in this house is awful. But I wouldn't change a day of it. So I've been very lucky. And I've been very lucky that we have wonderful kids, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the kids, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we have four. My oldest daughter, I can't believe that she turned 40. You know, I never thought I would live to see 40, let alone have a daughter of 40. She is a social worker. And she's the executive director of an organization called CHIRS. That is an organization that deals with people who have a brain injury. She's married, also to a social worker. And they have two children. My, my oldest grandson is David. And Darren is the younger one. He is three and a half, going on four. Three and a half going on 50-- bargains with me all the time. And my next daughter is Bonnie, and she's a dentist. And she's married and has a little boy, Ari. My third daughter is Joyce, and she works for the federal government. And in fact, right now she's very worried about her job, with all the, the downsizing. And she is married to Jeff, who is a bookkeeper for a company. And they have a little boy whose name is Aaron. And my son, Mark, is an engineer with an MBA and is working for Magna International. And he's married to Iris, who is an orthodontist and very anxious to put braces on my teeth. [LAUGHING] Very anxious.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we better mention the first two son-in-laws, as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I didn't do that? Danny, and he's a social worker. I think I said that. Or that he's an engineer with Hydro.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they are-- they're good kids. They're good kids. They tried hard. They did well at school. They strive for good things. And the little kids, I just love them. They're wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: It shows on your face.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they come to the cottage, and they cause lots of trouble. And they catch fish, and I have to take them off the hook. And I have to put worms on the hook. And we enjoy them very much, both Howard and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I take you back to your mother for a moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I, I think that maybe one should share some of the difficult times, as well. I think something that I have never shared was when she died. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think when, when she died in Bergen-Belsen, I didn't believe she was dead. And I didn't want to believe that she was dead. I, I shook her, and I shook her. [CRYING] And the people there-- the, the women-- said, she's dead. Don't shake her. She won't wake up. And then they took her out, and they dragged her out. And they put her outside of the barrack, outside of the barrack. [CRYING] And, you know, in those days, if you had any clothes on, they took off the clothes because someone else could use it. And they asked me to take off the clothes, and I wouldn't. [SOBS] And then they took her out, but she still had the nylon stockings. [SOBS] And they sent me out to take off the nylon stockings, and I wouldn't do it. [SOBS] And then they just threw her out to the wagon and took her away. And I-- [CRYING] I think the only time that I think about it is when I say [NON-ENGLISH]. And I think to myself, you know, [SOBBING] she's in one of those common graves in Bergen-Belsen, with-- [SOBBING] where you see all the time on TV and all. And, and I think maybe that's why I very seldom watch some of those documentaries. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I really have never shared that because it's just so difficult. [SOBBING] But I'll never forget how they sent me out. They said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Go and get the, the, the silk stockings. They'll keep you warm. I said, but she will be cold. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And it's, it's those kind of memories.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's, it's, it's memories even in camp. My father was gone already a long time. And, and there was a man who sort of took an interest in her. And I remember hating him as a kid. I, I-- you know, why does he come to my mother? And those are the kind of things, things that I think comprise life. And one of my things-- I mean, I'm 63 years old. And I think in retrospect, she was a young woman. She was younger than my children today. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] How did these people feel emotionally and, and, and, and sexually and everything without husbands, without men, men without women? I mean, I don't know. You don't think about it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So there are things, bits and pieces. My mother used to, to read to me in camp. The bits and pieces of paper where she would show me the letters, where she would make me work my finger so that my fingers would be agile to play piano. Because she was a pianist. She studied at the Conservatory of Vienna. And, and she was a-- she was a distinguished woman in her own right. That's-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's good that we are living in Canada. It's good that we are-- can look to the future. It's better than maybe to look at the past, but I think it's important to remember the past. But-- And then, you know, whenever I hear about all of the racism, and I see it all, and all, it's very scary. Very scary. Because it can happen again. It can happen again. It is happening again.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know that you've had a very active political career. I use politics in that sense.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is politics!

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, can you share that part of your life with the rest of us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was always involved with my children's education, and I always wanted them to achieve. And I was very involved with the schools. And I was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was a bit of a troublemaker where, I wanted them to achieve, and not only scholastically, but I wanted also this society to strive for things that we were lacking. And so I became an activist, an educational activist. And in 1977, people within the community urged me to run for the position of school trustee. And I ran against maybe five people, and I came in very much ahead. And I became a school trustee with the North York Board of Education. I have been now a trustee for 18 years. I've held many positions. I was budget chief. I was a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] member of many, many committees involved in anti-racism education. I was-- I am one that developed and initiated the development of the Holocaust curriculum. Very much involved in what's called the Role of the Reader, so that our teachers can teach material that has different stereotypes in it and teach it in such a way that people understand the stereotyping of if. I was chair of the board for two years-- the Board of Education-- which is on par with my friend, Mel Lastman, with whom I work closely. I was a member of Metro School Board. As chair of the board, worked with the Ministry in development of some curriculum material.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's been 18 years of, of, I would say, pretty hard work. As chair of the board, I was busy between 12 and 18 hours a day on board business. Some people don't realize that it's quite a job because we make decisions that are based on not only legislation but also regulations that are developed by the board. Which means the reg-- the, the, the regulations that I helped develop.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on top of it, there are 132 schools in North York. So there's an awful lot of schools where you're in charge of principal changes. You're in charge of some curricular matters. You're in charge of hiring and firing and negotiations. And that's not a-- not a minor kind job. And it's been very interesting. It's been very interesting. And, and at every election-- well, I came in twice reclamation where no one ran against me. And in some of the elections, I've had some very prestigious people running against me so that also-- I guess it means to me that I was still chosen. Makes me feel good, anyway. I can fool myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: No need to fool yourself. What's this I heard about a recent award that you received for excellence? Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was one of my schools and myself received a Quality of Excellence Award from the Canadian government. And it's a-- it's a-- it's a nice prestigious award. But I've received other awards. I received an award from [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some newspaper as a-- for public work. I received an award from Hadassah, as, as-- oh, what is it called?

speaker: interviewer
text: It'll come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, over the years there have been a few. There have been few acknowledgements about the work I have done.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your, your one secret to how you can do all that and bring up such a wonderful family at the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know, you know. They always say give a busy person a job and they'll do it. Somehow, I manage. Right now I'm very busy. This week I'm very busy with Hadassah. Hadassah bazaar is on Wednesday, and I already booked off two days. And you, you manage. You manage. Somehow you manage. I'm sure that along the way if I would think if something suffered, something does suffer. My house isn't always up to date the way I would like it to be. My cooking isn't always done. But I do maintain a kosher home. My family comes for dinner most Fridays, and that's a big group of people. My children are good. They help, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: May I take this moment to sincerely thank you for sharing your life with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much, Simon. It-- I would say it was a pleasure, but it's not a pleasure to share that kind of and that part of life. But on the other hand, it's, it's interesting that one can come out of darkness into light. And I hope that there will be light for our children and there will be good life for all of us. And of course, peace in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather, Yoachim Weinstock. And they lived in Brody. I think the picture looks like it might have been taken in Brody. And it's definitely before the war picture. He doesn't look too young, so I would imagine that the picture might have been sometime in the 1930s, late 1938, '38 or so. This is also my grandfather, Yoachim Weinstock. And it is amazing that as a young man, even though he was Jewish, he was able to serve in the Austrian army. I never realized that Jews served in the Austrian army, as well. But it seems that people living in Austria and in Brody and Galicia did serve in the army. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my grandmother, again, my maternal grandmother. And her name is-- was Dora Weinstock. I don't recall her maiden name. And I'm not sure where the picture was taken. But she's obviously a woman of maybe 30. And as far as I see, she has tremendous resemblance to my mother. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture that has my mother on it, and her brother, [? Salo ?], or Alexander, and my grandmother, Dora Weinstock. I have no idea when this picture could've been taken, but my mother looks about a girl like-- a girl of 10 or 12. And there is quite a resemblance, a family resemblance, in all of them. And there's also a resemblance between my mother and my children. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] By looking at the picture, I would place it around 1935, 1936. In the middle, it's myself, and I look like I might be about four years old. On my left, the first lady is my grandmother, and my grandfather next to her. Then on the immediate left is my, my uncle and my aunt, my mother's brother. And then my mom and my dad on the right. I'm not quite sure where the picture was taken, but since the whole family is there, I would presume that it might've been at some family-- special family occasion. I also presume that it's prior to the birth of my brother. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my late brother. His name was Julius Biller. And right in the beginning of the war in 1941, we heard no more from him. In fact, we are quite sure that he was burned alive. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The little girl is myself, and on the left is my mother, Helen Biller Weinstock. And next to her is her father, my grandfather, Yoachim Weinstock, and Dora Weinstock, my grandmother. And I think that the picture might have been taken either in Iwonicz, which we-- a place we used to go on holidays, or Zakopane, or one of the German resort places, because we did spend a lot of times-- a lot of summertimes in some of the resorts. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My mom and dad, Helen Biller and [INAUDIBLE]. And I really don't know where the picture was taken. It looks like a summer picture. Might have been taken in Brody. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think this is a very pretty little girl. And it is myself. And it looks again like it was taken in Iwonicz. One of the summer holidays, it looks like. There is most likely a, a resort of some sort behind, behind the picture. That, too, is a picture that was taken before the war. And it's just a little girl. And what is very noticeable on it and of interest to me is that I have earrings. And I know that my ears were pierced before the war. And I had earrings in those days. And I, in fact, didn't quite have to have my ears re-pierced after the war when I was wearing earrings. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In 1945, there was a small group of children that survived Bergen-Belsen. These children were brought to Sweden because we were very sick. And we were looked after in hospitals and hostels. What you see here are mostly nurses with the remnants of the children, of some of the children, that survived Bergen-Belsen. On the right, the first girl in the back is myself. And that's already-- by that time already, my hair grew back, and I already look like a little girl. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is our wedding picture. And it was November the 4th, 1951 when we got married. And we've been married now 43 years, and I would say we should continue in good health for many more together. I also would like to thank you, thank the people who were here and did the videotaping, and Simon, and everyone who contributed to this. And I hope that it'll serve a good purpose for posterity.
